title: The B. Team

jokes.text: Michael takes a job at a company that drives a car photographing every private residence — but the show blurs out the Google Street View car and logo, with the narrator saying 'we're just going to blur it anyway' and Michael told to 'something' it.
jokes.setup: Michael is driving a car for a mysterious tech company that photographs private residences.
jokes.punchline: The show can't legally show Google's car/logo, so they lean into the blur as a meta joke — 'Save it, we're just going to blur it anyway' and 'all you have to do is "something" it.'
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly self-aware joke that turns a real legal/clearance problem into a comedy bit. The show acknowledges it can't say 'Google' and makes that censorship itself the punchline, compounding with the narrator's winking 'all you have to do is "something" it.' High originality for finding comedy in real-world production constraints.

jokes.text: I'm used to a car with some stares. Albeit one that had trouble negotiating low-hanging obstacles.
jokes.setup: Michael is told the Google car will get some 'stares' from passersby.
jokes.punchline: The narrator references Michael's previous company car — the stair car — with the deadpan euphemism 'trouble negotiating low-hanging obstacles,' recalling the running stair-car gag from earlier seasons.
jokes.explanation: A dense, economical callback — 'stares' becomes 'stairs,' and 'low-hanging obstacles' is a perfectly deadpan description of the stair car's infamous banana stand/overpass collisions. Rewards long-time viewers enormously and is delivered with complete straight-faced efficiency.

jokes.text: This is going to be low. / I hooked it. — Visual: the Google camera car knocking into a low-hanging obstacle.
jokes.setup: After the stair-car callback about low-hanging obstacles, Michael is now in the Google car.
jokes.punchline: The Google car immediately hits a low-hanging obstacle, completing the joke visually — 'I hooked it.'
jokes.explanation: The physical payoff of the stair-car/low-hanging-obstacle setup. The show sets up the joke verbally and then immediately pays it off visually, with 'I hooked it' as a casual, perfect button. Excellent structural escalation from the preceding lines.

jokes.text: But it's also a company that won't let us show the car that takes those pictures. In fairness to them, it is their property.
jokes.setup: The narrator explains Michael drives a car for a tech company that photographs residences.
jokes.punchline: The narrator acknowledges they can't show the car (Google), adding 'In fairness to them, it is their property' — a deadpan corporate apology mid-narration.
jokes.explanation: A continuation of the meta-Google bit, the narrator apologizing to Google in-character as a legal disclaimer is absurdly funny. Slightly weaker than the initial blur joke because it's a follow-up, but 'in fairness to them, it is their property' is a great deadpan corporate genuflection.

jokes.text: Barry in front of a jury, offering to tell Ron Howard to 'go (BLEEP) himself' and 'shove it up his (BLEEP)' while apologizing to the jury: 'Sorry, everybody. I'm an attorney, too.'
jokes.setup: Barry calls Michael from what sounds like a casual situation.
jokes.punchline: Barry is revealed to be in front of a jury and casually offers profane legal advice mid-trial, then awkwardly tells the jury he's also an attorney.
jokes.explanation: Classic Barry Zuckerkorn incompetence. The reveal that he's conducting a trial while making a personal call — and his completely oblivious 'Sorry, everybody. I'm an attorney, too' — is perfect character comedy. Only Barry would need to clarify his profession to a jury mid-trial.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Apparently, he directed a movie called Cocoon.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Barry got any other information about why Ron Howard wants to meet.
jokes.punchline: Barry's only intel on Ron Howard is 'Apparently, he directed a movie called Cocoon' — as if this is obscure breaking news.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical. Barry's complete ignorance of one of Hollywood's most famous directors, delivered with the gravity of a hot tip, is pure character comedy. 'Apparently' does enormous work — it makes Ron Howard sound like a rumor Barry just heard. One of the most quotable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: The Bob Loblaw Law Bomb sequence: 'If he can't reach, this trial's a breach.' / 'That's a low blow, Loblaw.' / 'A Bob Loblaw Law Bomb.' / 'Ahhh.'
jokes.setup: Barry demonstrates in court that he can't reach the school gate latch — 'I can't reach the Chachi' — while on tippy-toes.
jokes.punchline: Bob Loblaw lands a rhyming courtroom zinger ('If he can't reach, this trial's a breach'), Barry calls it a 'low blow, Loblaw,' then escalates to 'A Bob Loblaw Law Bomb' — a tongue-twisting portmanteau that earns a groan-laugh from the room.
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode. 'Bob Loblaw Law Bomb' is a tongue-twisting phonetic masterpiece that builds through escalating wordplay — the rhyming couplet, the 'low blow Loblaw' retort, and then the compound portmanteau that synthesizes the character's name, his profession, and the slang term into one perfectly constructed phrase. It's layered, original, character-specific, and became a genuine cultural reference point for AD fans. Comparable in quality to the vasectomy anchor example.

jokes.text: Barry: 'I can't reach the Chachi.' / 'Hey, should I try tippy-toe? Look, I'm on tippy-toes.'
jokes.setup: Barry is demonstrating in court that the defendant couldn't reach the school gate latch.
jokes.punchline: Barry himself can't reach it, announces he can't reach 'the Chachi' (a reference to his own shortness/Scott Baio), then earnestly asks if he should try tippy-toes and does so.
jokes.explanation: Barry's complete lack of self-awareness — not only is his client too short, Barry himself is too short — is classic character comedy. 'The Chachi' is an AD-specific bit of slang that adds texture. The tippy-toe physical bit is a nice visual escalation.

jokes.text: 'That's a low blow, Loblaw. A Bob Loblaw Law Bomb.'
jokes.setup: Barry has just delivered his 'Law Bomb' in court.
jokes.punchline: The opposing counsel (Bob Loblaw, a recurring character from earlier seasons) counters with 'That's a low blow, Loblaw' — an alliterative tongue-twister — which is then completed as 'A Bob Loblaw Law Bomb,' creating a spectacular piece of wordplay.
jokes.explanation: This is among the finest pieces of wordplay in the entire series. 'A Bob Loblaw Law Bomb' is a nearly perfect sentence: it's an alliterative, rhythmic tongue-twister that also functions as a logical punchline to Barry's 'Law Bomb' bit. It requires no setup beyond the character name — the name IS the joke. It rewards prior knowledge of Bob Loblaw but works even cold. This is what a 9+ looks like: layered, quotable, original, and structurally flawless.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael drove to the North American headquarters of Imagine Entertainment, the modest film, television and streaming colossus'
jokes.setup: Setting up Imagine Entertainment as a major studio
jokes.punchline: 'modest... colossus' — an oxymoron delivered deadpan by the narrator
jokes.explanation: A quick, clean narrator irony joke. Functional but not exceptional — the oxymoron is well-placed but not a surprise given the narrator's established voice.

jokes.text: 'Ankles' means 'leaves' in Variety, Michael.
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at Imagine and sees a sign or headline that says something involving 'ankles.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator flatly explains that Hollywood trade-speak turns departure into body parts — 'Ankles means leaves in Variety.'
jokes.explanation: A crisp, insider Hollywood joke about trade paper jargon. The narrator's flat delivery — treating 'ankles means leaves' as obvious common knowledge — captures Michael's fish-out-of-water status in Hollywood perfectly. Economical and believable.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'I'm a D girl.' Michael: 'No, I don't want to see them.' / Kitty: 'It means development. I'm a movie executive.'
jokes.setup: Kitty announces she's a 'D girl' at Imagine.
jokes.punchline: Michael — conditioned by Kitty's previous behavior of flashing him — immediately assumes she means something sexual, cutting her off before she can explain she means 'development executive.'
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed callback that only works if you know Kitty's character. Michael's reflexive 'No, I don't want to see them' lands perfectly because it's a completely rational response given Kitty's history. The misdirection is organic, character-integrated, and earns its laugh through established behavioral logic rather than cheap shock. One of the stronger jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And she'd proven as loyal to him as she was to her former boss, Michael's father. But without the quote-unquote benefits. Also, Imagine provided no health benefits.'
jokes.setup: The narrator notes Kitty is loyal to Ron Howard as she was to George Sr.
jokes.punchline: 'Without the quote-unquote benefits' implies she had a sexual relationship with George Sr. Then 'Also, Imagine provided no health benefits' takes 'benefits' literally as a second punchline — a bait-and-switch on the word.
jokes.explanation: This is elegant double-meaning wordplay: 'benefits' meaning sexual perks → pivots to employment benefits with a deadpan 'Also.' The economy is exceptional — the joke is constructed in two sentences using one word doing double duty. The narrator's dry pivot from innuendo to HR policy is a signature Arrested Development move.

jokes.text: The narrator explaining Kitty's loyalty to Ron Howard: 'And she'd proven as loyal to him as she was to her former boss, Michael's father. But without the quote-unquote benefits. Also, Imagine provided no health benefits.'
jokes.setup: The narrator notes Kitty has transferred her loyalty from George Bluth Sr. to Ron Howard.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'quote-unquote benefits' implies a sexual arrangement with George Sr., then pivots to 'Also, Imagine provided no health benefits' — treating the two entirely different kinds of 'benefits' as a dry list.
jokes.explanation: A classic AD narrator move — setting up a dark implication ('quote-unquote benefits') and then immediately deflating it with a mundane HR complaint about health insurance. The double meaning of 'benefits' doing all the heavy lifting, delivered with complete deadpan economy. Smart and tight.

jokes.text: Michael: 'You're probably going to call me a pig for this, too, but are you sure that you meant to say "knew"?' Narrator: 'She did.' — followed by extended explanation of what Kitty actually blew.
jokes.setup: Michael questions Kitty's use of the past tense 'knew' when referring to the people who got her the job.
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms she did mean 'knew' — and then reveals the only thing Kitty 'blew' was smoke up the skirt of the young woman who hired her (Maeby), making the implied sexual joke pivot to a workplace politics joke instead.
jokes.explanation: A double-misdirection joke: Michael's question implies the obvious sexual reading of 'blew,' the narrator confirms the past tense ('She did'), then reveals that the only thing Kitty blew was 'smoke up the skirt' — itself a euphemism, but for flattery, not sex. Masterfully layered with AD's signature narrator wit.

jokes.text: Maeby, a 17-year-old, hiring Kitty as an assistant and asking her to buy booze and 'smack or hash or Special K.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is revealed to have been working as a film executive, and Kitty was her assistant.
jokes.punchline: Maeby, established as a minor, immediately leverages her power position to have her adult assistant procure alcohol and illegal drugs — listing them casually as workplace perks.
jokes.explanation: The joke is perfectly character-specific to Maeby — the teenage con artist who climbed to executive status. Having her adult assistant buy drugs for her while she's technically a minor is the natural extension of her character arc and works as dark comedy.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'You know where all the bodies are buried.' Kitty: 'And I even helped bury some of them.' Narrator: 'Maeby was only 17 at the time.'
jokes.setup: Kitty says she knows where the 'bodies are buried' as a metaphor for family secrets
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms the metaphor might not be entirely metaphorical — 'Maeby was only 17 at the time' adds a disturbing context to Kitty's literal statement
jokes.explanation: The narrator's interjection makes a metaphorical phrase literal — a signature AD technique. The timing of 'Maeby was only 17' as a parenthetical adds both darkness and comedy through implication.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And the project got thrown on the back burner.' — Visual: a literal project (script?) on a stove burner.
jokes.setup: The narrator is explaining that Kitty sabotaged Maeby's project while she was away.
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'thrown on the back burner' is a cliché — the show likely literalizes it visually, consistent with AD's habit of making idioms physical.
jokes.explanation: AD frequently literalizes idioms as visual gags (see: 'put it on the back burner'). Inferred from transcript context. Competent AD formula but not distinctive enough to rank high.

jokes.text: Only Jerry Bruckheimer's building was technically taller. But who wants to be south of Wilshire?
jokes.setup: Kitty is explaining that Imagine owns the second-largest building in Beverly Hills.
jokes.punchline: The narrator clarifies Bruckheimer's building is taller but dismisses it with insider Hollywood real estate snobbery: 'But who wants to be south of Wilshire?'
jokes.explanation: A solid insider Hollywood joke about real estate pecking order. The condescension toward south-of-Wilshire feels authentically Beverly Hills. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'But first, Michael would have to pass a few little Rons.' — Visual: multiple Ron Howard lookalike assistants.
jokes.setup: Michael is heading to meet Ron Howard.
jokes.punchline: The narrator announces Michael must first pass 'a few little Rons' — implying Ron Howard has surrounded himself with diminutive doppelgängers as assistants.
jokes.explanation: A delightfully absurd bit — the visual of multiple Ron Howard lookalikes as gatekeepers is both surreal and a sharp satire of Hollywood ego. 'We're never going to beat the original' from one of the little Rons is a perfectly self-aware button.

jokes.text: Little Ron: 'Hey, nice try, mister. We're never going to beat the original.' Michael: 'Well, that's what I've been saying.'
jokes.setup: Michael encounters the Ron Howard lookalike assistants.
jokes.punchline: The little Ron's self-deprecating admission ('We're never going to beat the original') is met with Michael's completely sincere agreement — he's been saying he can't measure up too, creating an accidental bond.
jokes.explanation: A nice comedic misunderstanding where Michael's self-deprecation accidentally aligns with the lookalike's Ron Howard envy. The double meaning works economically.

jokes.text: Ron Howard is finishing 'casting this Andy Griffith Show thing' and has been reading about it for 40 years.
jokes.setup: Ron Howard, famously a child star on The Andy Griffith Show, is now directing a movie version of it.
jokes.punchline: Ron says he's 'been reading about it for, like, 40 years' — a wry acknowledgment that he, of all people, would have unique standing to make this film, played completely straight.
jokes.explanation: Works because the real Ron Howard IS Opie Taylor — so hearing him say he's been following the Andy Griffith project for 40 years is meta and mildly absurdist. The character integration is near-perfect because it's about his real biography.

jokes.text: Ron Howard: 'Hey, let's go inside the LEM. You want to? Is this the one that landed on the moon?' Ron: 'On a soundstage.' Michael: 'Oh, right, from Apollo 13.' Ron: 'No, no, 1969.'
jokes.setup: Ron Howard invites Michael into a lunar excursion module (LEM), which Michael assumes is a prop from Apollo 13.
jokes.punchline: Ron corrects him — it's from 1969, because the actual moon landing was faked on the soundstage of Gentle Ben, and he and his brother hid in the rafters and saw the whole thing.
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliantly constructed bit. Ron Howard, as himself, casually reveals that the 1969 moon landing was faked on the set of Gentle Ben (a show he actually appeared on as a child) while he and Clint Howard hid in the rafters. It's historically absurdist, character-specific (only Ron Howard could plausibly say this), takes a sharp left turn from Apollo 13 prop humor, and is delivered with complete sincerity. The 'it's a national secret' setup makes it land even harder.

jokes.text: Ron: 'So, NASA did go to the moon in '71. That one was real. But in '69, they weren't ready, so they faked the whole thing on the soundstage of Gentle Ben.'
jokes.setup: Ron has just revealed the 1969 moon landing conspiracy.
jokes.punchline: He clarifies that '71 was real — making the conspiracy theory oddly specific and partial, with the confidence of someone who was there.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the moon conspiracy bit into specific detail ('That one was real') with Ron Howard as the eyewitness is exceptional. The absurdist logic is internally consistent and the character specificity (Gentle Ben was a real show Ron Howard appeared in) makes it far more than a generic conspiracy joke.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Whoa! Hang on, are you kidding me? I'm a Phoenix. I've never met anyone else in person that also goes there.' Ron: 'I guess that's the downside of going to college online.' Michael: 'Well, I just got a sick aunt down there.'
jokes.setup: Ron mentions he's been going to Phoenix, and Michael gets excited thinking Ron also attends University of Phoenix (the online college).
jokes.punchline: Ron clarifies he literally goes to Phoenix, Arizona — and Michael's embarrassed explanation ('I just got a sick aunt down there') makes it clear the misunderstanding was entirely his own delusion.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured misdirection. Michael's enthusiasm — 'I'm a Phoenix!' — followed by Ron's deadpan reality check ('the downside of going to college online') reveals both Michael's insecurity about his education AND the comedy of him attending University of Phoenix while trying to impress a Hollywood legend. Character-specific and economical.

jokes.text: Ron Howard's movies sourced from random images: Splash from a counterfeit Hockney, Da Vinci Code from a photo, Willow from a Soft 'n Snuggly coupon.
jokes.setup: Ron tells Michael that most of his movies are based on still photographs he finds inspiring.
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms this with increasingly absurd examples: Splash from a fake Hockney, Da Vinci Code from an unspecified photo, and Willow — a grand fantasy epic — from a Soft 'n Snuggly diaper coupon.
jokes.explanation: An escalating absurdist list with a perfect button — Willow, a Tolkien-esque epic, inspired by a diaper coupon. Each entry gets more ridiculous. The joke also works as satire of Hollywood origin stories and creative mythology. Character-specific to Ron Howard's real filmography.

jokes.text: Ron: 'Plus I've been dying to figure out a way to do something about the market crash ever since my partner, Brian Grazer, was tipped off that it was three months away from happening.'
jokes.setup: Ron is explaining his interest in Michael's story — the family, the market crash.
jokes.punchline: In passing, Ron reveals that Brian Grazer had insider knowledge of the 2008 market crash ('tipped off that it was three months away from happening') — essentially admitting his partner may have committed securities fraud, treated as a totally normal detail.
jokes.explanation: A casually dropped bomb — Ron Howard's business partner committed what sounds like insider trading — slipped in as a throwaway detail. The joke is that Ron mentions it completely without embarrassment, as if it's a fun anecdote. Dark, sharp, and perfectly underplayed.

jokes.text: Ron: 'Your wife is dying. You're trying to hold your family together.' Michael: 'Oh, gosh, no, no. My wife died years before any of this.' Ron: 'I think it's a lot more fun if we see her die.'
jokes.setup: Ron pitches Michael's life story to him, getting key facts wrong.
jokes.punchline: When Michael corrects that his wife already died, Ron — purely for cinematic purposes — suggests 'I think it's a lot more fun if we see her die.' His clinical enthusiasm for exploiting Michael's tragedy is both horrifying and hilarious.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly dark Hollywood satire joke. Ron Howard re-killing Michael's already-dead wife because 'it's a lot more fun' is a razor-sharp skewering of the Hollywood tendency to dramatize tragedy regardless of actual facts. The casualness of 'I think it's a lot more fun' is what makes it devastating. Character-specific to Ron Howard's persona as a sentimentalizing Hollywood director.

jokes.text: Michael assuming 'my girl Rebel' refers to Ron Howard's mistress — talking conspiratorially in the LEM — while Ron is actually referring to his daughter.
jokes.setup: Ron mentions 'my girl Rebel' would be perfect for the part of the dying wife. Michael (and the audience) assumes this is Ron's mistress.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Ron is talking about his actual daughter. Michael's conspiratorial 'I can see why you're telling me in the LEM, huh?' lands as a cringe-funny misread.
jokes.explanation: A structurally excellent long-form misdirection. The show seeds the ambiguity naturally, allows Michael to misread it hilariously ('I can see why you're telling me in the LEM'), and then has the narrator deliver the correction. Also sets up the episode's romantic climax. The rewatch bonus is enormous because everything Michael says in the LEM scene reads completely differently.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm not one to judge. I'm sure you've all got girls up here in Hollywood.' Ron: 'Brian's got two boys.'
jokes.setup: Michael, assuming Ron is confessing a mistress, charitably says he doesn't judge Hollywood men with 'girls.'
jokes.punchline: Ron, completely missing (or ignoring) the implication, notes that Brian Grazer has two boys — a completely literal non-response that deepens Michael's misunderstanding.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the mistress misunderstanding. Ron's completely innocent 'Brian's got two boys' — meant to correct Michael's 'you've all got girls' — accidentally sounds like Ron is confirming the mistress culture while clarifying Brian prefers boys. The economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Ron: 'How do you tell Ed Harris that he's simply not a Barney?'
jokes.setup: Ron mentions he has a tough meeting coming up.
jokes.punchline: The tough meeting is telling Ed Harris — one of Hollywood's most respected actors — that he's not right for the role of Barney Fife in the Andy Griffith film.
jokes.explanation: The specific pairing of Ed Harris — intense, theatrical, Academy Award nominated — with Barney Fife — bumbling, neurotic, comic — is perfectly chosen for maximum absurdity. The matter-of-fact framing ('How do you tell Ed Harris...') respects the situation's gravity while exposing its absurdity.

jokes.text: Michael is given a producer card that accidentally makes him a producer. He's delighted. Narrator: 'Health plan is not another one of the perks, by the way. No health plan.'
jokes.setup: Michael is thrilled to discover he's accidentally become a movie producer.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately deflates the moment by noting there's no health plan — a running gag across the episode.
jokes.explanation: A competent running gag. The no-health-plan joke recurs throughout and earns a mild laugh each time. Not innovative, but serves the episode's rhythm.

jokes.text: Barry: 'It sounds like Ron Howard is casting with his (BLEEP).' Michael: 'Well, it is hard to believe, but I guess they've all got their mistresses up here in showbiz.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells Barry that Ron is casting an actress he's romantically involved with.
jokes.punchline: Barry's blunt anatomical assessment is followed by Michael's sympathetic take — not realizing Rebel is Ron's daughter, not mistress — continuing the running misunderstanding.
jokes.explanation: Continues the running misunderstanding effectively. Barry's crude assessment is in-character and the irony of Michael defending Hollywood casting couch culture when Ron is just proud of his daughter is nicely layered.

jokes.text: George Bluth Sr.'s father never signed birthday cards — only gave money orders — because he claimed he 'didn't have a signature.' Barry rhymes: 'Oh! If you don't sign, you will be fine.'
jokes.setup: Michael wonders if his father would sign a release — noting that getting a signature from him has always been impossible.
jokes.punchline: The reason is revealed: George Sr. claims he doesn't have a signature, which is why all presents were money orders. Barry celebrates this legal non-signing with another rhyming couplet.
jokes.explanation: A layered joke that works on multiple levels: George Sr.'s lifelong aversion to paper trails (a character-consistent behavior for a man who's been convicted of fraud), the absurdity of 'not having a signature,' and Barry's gleeful legal spin ('If you don't sign, you will be fine'). Character integration is excellent.

jokes.text: Michael bumping into Rebel Alley — literally — in a meet-cute so clichéd she was literally auditioning for a scene exactly like it.
jokes.setup: Rebel has just failed an audition for a meet-cute cliché scene where a guy and girl bump into each other.
jokes.punchline: She walks out and immediately has the exact scenario happen with Michael — unknowingly living the scene she just bombed at.
jokes.explanation: Structurally elegant. The show sets up the trope critique ('these ridiculous meet-cute clichés'), has Rebel fail at performing one, and then has life immediately deliver the real version. The meta commentary on romantic comedy conventions is sharp, and the irony is perfectly constructed. The structural payoff is excellent.

jokes.text: Michael: 'My deceased wife had red hair.' Rebel: 'Yep, garbage like that.'
jokes.setup: Michael is listing romantic cliché lines the woman in the scene might say.
jokes.punchline: He says 'My deceased wife had red hair' as an example of a garbage cliché — not realizing his own dead wife is a real plot point AND Rebel has red hair, making it accidentally sincere and accidentally a pickup line.
jokes.explanation: This is the best-constructed individual joke in the episode. Michael is listing what he thinks are absurd romantic clichés — 'My deceased wife had red hair' — but it's simultaneously TRUE (his wife is dead), SPECIFIC TO HIM as a character, and directed at a red-haired woman. He's unintentionally delivering the sincere version of the cliché he thinks he's mocking. Rebel dismissing it as 'garbage like that' when it's his actual life is devastating and hilarious. The character integration is as close to perfect as this episode gets. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor — possibly better.

jokes.text: Rebel: 'And it's so unbelievable. Like, they never get each other's names.' — and then neither Michael nor Rebel ever get each other's names throughout the scene.
jokes.setup: Rebel critiques how unrealistic meet-cute movies are because they never get each other's names.
jokes.punchline: The scene continues with Michael and Rebel never exchanging names, proving her point while being in the middle of it.
jokes.explanation: A structurally perfect joke that extends across the episode — Rebel critiques the name-amnesia trope and then the show commits to it, leading to the running gag of Michael desperately trying to find her name. The payoff (her tattoo revealing her name after they've slept together) is a callback to this setup that makes it one of the episode's sustained best jokes.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm a movie producer. Sorry. I'm a movie... Here, I've got... I've got proof here. Huh?' — showing his accidental producer card.
jokes.setup: Rebel jokes 'Where's a movie producer when you need one?' — sarcastically, to the universe.
jokes.punchline: Michael was literally just made an accidental producer and frantically produces his card as evidence, delivered with a desperately eager 'Huh?'
jokes.explanation: A solid situational comedy moment — the universe delivers a literal answer to Rebel's rhetorical question, and Michael's eagerness to prove his legitimacy with a card he just got is funny character comedy.

jokes.text: The narrator: 'It wasn't until Michael had walked two blocks past the California Pizza Kitchen when this happened. Hey, I met a girl today and her name is (BLEEP).'
jokes.setup: After the meet-cute, Michael realizes he never got the woman's name.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals he never got her name — and his realization is bleeping where her name would be, extending the running gag of not knowing her name.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful structural payoff — the narrator bleeping the woman's name where it would appear confirms both that Michael really doesn't know it AND extends the show's commitment to the meet-cute parody. Using a bleep for an unknown name (not a profanity) is an original formal choice.

jokes.text: Michael searching headshots for the unnamed woman: 'Nothing? She was super-pretty, red hair...'
jokes.setup: Michael goes back to Imagine trying to find the woman through headshots.
jokes.punchline: His search parameters are 'super-pretty, red hair' — a comically insufficient description for finding someone in a city of actors.
jokes.explanation: A functional continuation of the no-name running gag. Not particularly clever but serves the plot progression.

jokes.text: George Sr. hiding in Lucille 2's apartment — then Michael points out he's in the wrong Lucille's apartment.
jokes.setup: Michael finds his father hiding somewhere, clearly trying to avoid someone.
jokes.punchline: He's in the wrong Lucille's apartment — he meant to go to Lucille Bluth's but ended up at Lucille Austero's (Lucille 2). Michael notes this might be evidence of his cheating.
jokes.explanation: A layered AD joke requiring knowledge of two characters both named Lucille. The implication that George Sr. can't keep his Lucilles straight — and Michael immediately connecting 'wrong apartment' to infidelity — is excellent character comedy that rewards series knowledge.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I just came back to get some suits, you know, so I can look like the uptight, dishonest, cheating boob that I am.' Michael: 'Well, I never said "boob."'
jokes.setup: George Sr. sarcastically explains why he returned.
jokes.punchline: Michael — instead of objecting to 'dishonest' or 'cheating' — objects only to the word 'boob,' implicitly confirming all the other descriptors are accurate.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest exchanges. Michael's selective objection — he won't fight 'dishonest' or 'cheating' as descriptors but draws the line at 'boob' — is a devastating character moment delivered with comic economy. The implicit confirmation of George's moral failures through Michael's one-word objection is exceptional writing.

jokes.text: Michael's prolonged pause while trying to think of a nice word for how George Sr. will be portrayed in the movie — nearly 30 seconds — before managing 'Nice.'
jokes.setup: Michael promises George Sr. can be made to 'seem very, very, uh, you know...' and then cannot think of a single positive quality to attribute.
jokes.punchline: After an agonizingly long pause, the only word he produces is 'Nice' — the most generic possible compliment — and George Sr. immediately tells him to 'Go to hell.'
jokes.explanation: A near-perfectly structured comedic beat. The setup is clear: Michael has to sell his father on being portrayed positively but cannot name a single positive thing about him. The ~27-second pause (from 'uh, you know...' at 13:41 to 'Nice' at 14:02) is the joke — the silence condemns George Sr. more completely than any insult could. 'Nice' as the desperate bottom-of-the-barrel compliment and George's immediate 'Go to hell' is a perfect button. This is among the best structural jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Explanation of why Michael's office ceiling is so low: Brian and Ron wanted taller ceilings, Bruckheimer blocked the building height, so Ron said '(BLEEP) you, Jerry' and lowered his floor. Brian then wanted his ceiling just a few inches taller than Ron's — causing Michael's office to be lowest of all.
jokes.setup: Michael notices the ceilings in his office are unusually low.
jokes.punchline: The explanation reveals a cascade of Hollywood ego: Bruckheimer's city council connections, Ron's spite move, and Brian's one-upmanship all combining to make Michael's office nearly uninhabitable — a physical manifestation of Hollywood hierarchy.
jokes.explanation: An escalating absurdist explanation that works on multiple levels: it's a Hollywood power dynamics satire, it's an escalation joke (each ceiling slightly lower than the one above), and it ends with Michael literally at the bottom — both physically and metaphorically. 'So Ron said, "(BLEEP) you, Jerry" and went lower with his floor' is a sharp, funny beat.

jokes.text: Michael's office has even lower ceilings than Ron's, because Brian wanted his ceiling higher than Ron's — an internal competition within the same company.
jokes.setup: Kitty explains the office layout follows from the ceiling feud.
jokes.punchline: 'Some internal competition. Kind of like between us.' — Michael's startled 'Wait, what?' reveals Kitty sees their dynamic as competitive too, a non-sequitur escalation.
jokes.explanation: A solid escalation of the building height gag. The additional layer of internal competition (Brian vs. Ron) and Kitty's abrupt pivot to 'kind of like between us' adds a funny non-sequitur element. The physical comedy of Michael having to duck in his own office is implied.

jokes.text: Kitty accuses Michael of 'starting with the casting couch already' — then: 'This is Imagine Entertainment, not the hot tub at Bruckheimer Tower. We make family movies, you (BLEEP) pig.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Kitty for a directory of actresses to find the red-haired woman.
jokes.punchline: Kitty assumes he's casting from a couch and defends Imagine's family values with 'We make family movies' — then immediately calls him a '(BLEEP) pig.'
jokes.explanation: Kitty's moral contradiction — defending family values while calling Michael a '(BLEEP) pig' — is very on-brand. The Bruckheimer hot tub reference adds a nice Hollywood-rivalry beat. Character integration is strong because this is exactly how Kitty operates.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers: 'Do you think anybody would be upset if one of these Crinch dolls took a walk?' — pocketing a doll while talking to Michael about the movie.
jokes.setup: Michael visits Carl Weathers to discuss the movie.
jokes.punchline: Carl casually asks if it's okay to steal a Crinch doll from the set — establishing his character's grift-adjacent nature while conducting a legitimate business conversation.
jokes.explanation: A solid Carl Weathers character joke — his penny-ante theft is perfectly in-character. The specificity of asking permission to steal ('do you think anybody would be upset') while already intending to take it is funny.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers: 'I know what you're thinking. "What part would I play in this?"' Michael: 'I didn't want to waste you on you.' 'In fact, I wasn't even going to talk to you about acting, but if you are game, boy, that would be great. Let's circle back to that.'
jokes.setup: Carl assumes Michael wants him to play himself in the movie.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'I didn't want to waste you on you' — delivered without irony — is a backhanded compliment of stunning economy, and then immediately pivoting to 'let's circle back to that' (meaning: we'll never discuss it again).
jokes.explanation: 'I didn't want to waste you on you' is a brilliant piece of dialogue — it sounds complimentary but is actually a subtle insult (playing yourself is beneath you). Delivered with complete sincerity, it's a perfectly economical line. The pivot to 'let's circle back to that' — Hollywood speak for 'I'll never mention this again' — is an excellent double beat.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers never got rights for the Scandalmakers episode about George Bluth Sr. — and so he made himself the subject of the final episode when caught.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Carl retained rights to the George Bluth story for Scandalmakers.
jokes.punchline: Carl never got rights for anything. When confronted about it for the final episode, his solution was to make himself the subject — which also presumably had no rights clearance.
jokes.explanation: The recursive logic of Carl solving a rights problem by creating another rights problem — with himself as the subject — is perfectly absurd. The callback to Scandalmakers is rewarding for series watchers.

jokes.text: The DeBrie collapse on the Scandalmakers set — 'Her heart stopped. She's (BLEEP) dying.' / 'Hey, Dave, you shaved, I like it.' / 'Please get the paddles!' / 'Hey, Mike. How was Ojai?'
jokes.setup: Carl is filming the final Scandalmakers episode, reenacting his own arrest.
jokes.punchline: A actress (DeBrie) collapses — heart stopped — and the crew around her treats the life-threatening emergency with the exact same casual energy they use for mundane small talk ('Hey, Dave, you shaved, I like it.' 'Hey Mike, how was Ojai?').
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of a cardiac emergency with the casual obliviousness of film-set small talk is a sharp bit of dark comedy. The crew greeting each other while someone is dying beside them is absurdist and lands because the contrast is so stark.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers: 'Man, you're as green as old lady Crinch. Come on, people don't go to movies to see rights. People go to movies to see actors.'
jokes.setup: Michael insists the movie needs proper rights since it's a true story.
jokes.punchline: Carl dismisses rights as irrelevant — the audience only cares about actors. This is simultaneously wrong (legally) and correct (commercially), and Carl delivers it with the authority of someone who has gotten away with this approach.
jokes.explanation: Carl Weathers as a cynical Hollywood veteran who has genuinely internalized 'better to ask forgiveness than beg for permission' — applied specifically to intellectual property — is a sharp character joke. 'People go to movies to see actors' is quotable.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Who knows my father better than Warden Gentles?' — hiring the prison warden as screenwriter.
jokes.setup: Carl tells Michael he needs a scriptwriter — Michael needs someone who knows the family.
jokes.punchline: Michael's logic leads him to the prison warden — who knew George Sr. as his prisoner — as the ideal screenwriter. The jump cut to the warden is genuinely surprising.
jokes.explanation: The logic of 'who knows my father best? His warden!' is absurdist but follows a perfectly coherent AD-internal logic. The reveal is funny and the warden's subsequent screenwriting pretensions make it better.

jokes.text: Warden Gentles: 'Give me an old Royal and a glass of Scotch, and I'll give you 250 pages where the lightning hits the tree.' Then self-corrects: 'Where the drop hits the pond.'
jokes.setup: Warden Gentles is pitching his screenwriting credentials.
jokes.punchline: His folksy metaphor collapses mid-delivery — 'lightning hits the tree' is wrong, he means 'drop hits the pond' — suggesting he's confusing (or mixing) inspirational writing clichés.
jokes.explanation: The warden's failed attempt at inspirational writer-speak — mixing metaphors and then confidently correcting himself — is a good character comedy beat. Shows he's absorbed the language of creative people without understanding it.

jokes.text: Warden Gentles: 'Then the yellow robot gets mad at the pink robot...' Michael interrupts: 'Correction. The purple robot.' Warden: 'Sorry, I'm back to back today.'
jokes.setup: Warden Gentles is pitching multiple story ideas, apparently to multiple clients.
jokes.punchline: He's already pitching a robot story (completely unrelated to the Bluth family movie) and can't keep his pitches straight. Michael corrects the robot color. The warden accepts this with 'I'm back to back today.'
jokes.explanation: The warden being 'back to back' with Hollywood meetings and mixing up his pitches — including a robot story he's apparently also developing — is a perfect absurdist moment. Michael correcting the robot color as though that's the main problem is a great straight-man beat.

jokes.text: Warden Gentles: 'You're not charring my tree, and... Yeah, Jerry's not gonna come off the boat for this one.'
jokes.setup: Michael pitches the Bluth family story to the warden.
jokes.punchline: The warden rejects it in Hollywood-speak he's clearly just adopted — 'You're not charring my tree' (likely a mangled metaphor) and 'Jerry's not gonna come off the boat' (whatever that means).
jokes.explanation: The warden's adopted Hollywood-speak becoming increasingly cryptic and mangled ('charring my tree,' 'Jerry's not gonna come off the boat') is a consistent character comedy bit. Works best as part of the running warden characterization.

jokes.text: Warden: 'Might I suggest bringing in someone younger to play the father? A Philip Seymour Hoffman type?' — then Michael immediately cuts to: 'And so, naturally, I thought of you' — speaking to Conan O'Brien.
jokes.setup: The warden suggests a 'Philip Seymour Hoffman type' to play the father.
jokes.punchline: The cut to Michael saying 'naturally, I thought of you' to Conan O'Brien — neither Philip Seymour Hoffman nor the right type — is a visual misdirection joke about Michael's loose definition of 'type.'
jokes.explanation: The jump cut from 'Philip Seymour Hoffman type' to Conan O'Brien is a perfectly timed visual joke. The logic of Michael considering Conan — a talk show host with no apparent resemblance to PSH — 'naturally' is absurdist and works especially well as a visual payoff.

jokes.text: Conan O'Brien: 'I'm a married man, so I don't really keep a directory of attractive young actresses around.' — responding to Michael asking about actresses.
jokes.setup: Michael asks Conan if he knows any attractive young actresses.
jokes.punchline: Conan ties his non-possession of an actress directory to his marital status — implying that unmarried men routinely maintain such directories.
jokes.explanation: A solid celebrity cameo joke — Conan playing a version of himself who finds the actress directory question completely normal except for his marital constraints. The implication is funny.

jokes.text: Conan: 'I mean, are you really planning on using her or is it like Conan with the girl writers?'
jokes.setup: Michael is discussing hiring writers for the movie.
jokes.punchline: Conan references his own show's alleged mistreatment of female writers — calling himself out in the third person — as a benchmark for performative inclusion.
jokes.explanation: Conan self-deprecatingly referencing his own show's gender diversity issues — using himself in the third person — is a sharp, self-aware joke with real bite. It works because Conan is doing it to himself, which gives it credibility the show couldn't achieve with a fictional character.

jokes.text: Conan: 'You came over for a chicken and ham-water dinner that my family threw to raise some funds for itself.' 'And they're finally getting around to making a movie of that, huh?' 'There's more to it than that.' Conan: 'That just saved me 12 bucks. I'm not going to see it.'
jokes.setup: Conan asks Michael to remind him what their connection is — Michael summarizes the Bluth family story.
jokes.punchline: Michael's elevator pitch — 'a young man trying to get out from under his domineering father while dealing with the slow death of his wife' — prompts Conan to say it 'saved me 12 bucks' because the description is so complete he doesn't need to see the movie.
jokes.explanation: 'That just saved me 12 bucks' is an excellent line — it reduces Michael's passionate family story to a movie plot so efficient the pitch IS the movie. The 'ham-water dinner' callback also rewards series viewers. Conan's deadpan delivery makes this land.

jokes.text: Conan to Andy Richter: 'Just 'cause I look at you when we're doing the show and ask you a question, doesn't mean you have to respond. Sometimes it's funny when you don't say anything, and the audience gets to think, "Hey, Andy really is stupid." Gets a big laugh.'
jokes.setup: Conan gives Andy a note about the show while Michael watches.
jokes.punchline: Conan's 'coaching' is to tell Andy to let audiences think he's actually stupid — i.e., not responding to questions makes him seem dumber, and Conan finds this funny/useful.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly dark behind-the-curtain joke about the Conan/Andy dynamic. Conan is essentially telling Andy that appearing stupid is a strategic choice they're making together — and that Conan finds audience contempt for Andy funny. The character specificity (it's the REAL Conan and REAL Andy doing this) makes it devastating. One of the best celebrity cameo jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Conan to Michael: 'Hey, you're new.' Michael: 'I've been here a year.' Conan: 'And you're funny, too. Let's get you set up in a writer's office. You can have Andy's if you don't mind the smell of bologna.'
jokes.setup: Conan mistakes Michael for a new staff member.
jokes.punchline: He immediately pivots to offering Michael Andy's office — further marginalizing Andy in front of him — and the detail of 'the smell of bologna' makes it both specific and sad.
jokes.explanation: The casual cruelty of Conan immediately offering Andy's office to a stranger — with the specific detail of 'the smell of bologna' implying Andy's low-class lunch habits — is both dark and specific. The joke works because it's the real Conan and real Andy, and Conan has just been coaching Andy on appearing stupid.

jokes.text: Andy Richter: 'I'm in.' — immediately agreeing to leave Conan's show and join Michael's movie.
jokes.setup: Conan has just offered Andy's office to a stranger in front of him.
jokes.punchline: Andy's immediate 'I'm in' — agreeing to join Michael's film project — is both logical (given the humiliation he just witnessed) and delivered with the perfect brevity of someone who has been waiting for this opportunity.
jokes.explanation: Two words, maximum impact. The entire Conan sequence has been building to Andy's humiliation — the stupid coaching, the office being given away — and his 'I'm in' is the perfect one-beat payoff. It's confident, immediate, and suggests Andy has been waiting for an exit. Economy is essentially perfect: you could not do more with fewer syllables.

jokes.text: The pitch meeting with Kitty's accidental slide show of her granddaughter's graduation: 'I thought that was playing in here.' / 'Sorry, it was a slide show of my granddaughter's... daughter's graduation from college.' Michael: 'Sure.' Kitty: 'High school.'
jokes.setup: The team assembles for a pitch meeting and someone has a slide show running.
jokes.punchline: Kitty's clarifications keep downgrading the granddaughter's achievement — college, then high school — as she realizes she's overstated the occasion.
jokes.explanation: A solid descending-escalation joke — the achievement keeps getting smaller as Kitty corrects herself. The granddaughter's graduation going from college to high school is a nice subtle moment.

jokes.text: Ron Howard: 'Andy Richter?' Andy: 'I'm out, God damn it, I'm out.' — Andy quits the moment Ron doesn't recognize him.
jokes.setup: Michael presents his assembled team to Ron Howard, including Andy Richter.
jokes.punchline: Ron Howard doesn't recognize Andy Richter — the man who worked alongside Conan for years — and Andy immediately quits the project, apparently the final indignity after the Conan office moment.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly timed callback to Andy's earlier humiliation. Ron Howard not recognizing him — the final insult — triggers an immediate exit that pays off the entire Conan/Andy sequence. 'I'm out, God damn it, I'm out' is both funny in isolation and satisfying as a callback resolution. Andy's consistent pattern of being dismissed makes this feel earned.

jokes.text: Ron: 'Well, everybody's got to start somewhere. As long as I don't end up there.' — re: Warden Gentles' writing credential of multiple episodes of Rocko's Modern Life.
jokes.setup: Michael introduces Warden Gentles as writer of 'multiple episodes of Rocko's Modern Life.'
jokes.punchline: Ron acknowledges the humble credential charitably ('everybody's got to start somewhere') then immediately reveals his actual concern: 'As long as I don't end up there.'
jokes.explanation: The pivot from diplomatic encouragement to self-protective concern is a nicely constructed two-beat joke. Ron Howard's immediate qualification of his own generosity is a sharp character note — he can't even fake support for a full sentence.

jokes.text: Ron: 'You know, I thought it was Cuba with the perfect Carl Weathers makeup.' Michael: 'No, no, Cuba doesn't have that kind of range.'
jokes.setup: Ron thinks he's seeing Cuba Gooding Jr. playing Carl Weathers.
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects him — it IS Carl Weathers — but explains that Cuba Gooding Jr. doesn't have 'that kind of range' to play Carl Weathers. This is a meta-Hollywood comment on typecasting and perceived actor limitations.
jokes.explanation: A callback to the 'waste Cuba on Carl' joke from earlier, now completing the loop: it's the real Carl, and Cuba Gooding Jr. lacks 'the range' to play him. The insulting inversion — saying the Oscar winner lacks range to play a B-list action star — is a genuinely sharp, surprising line. 'Cuba doesn't have that kind of range' is immediately quotable.

jokes.text: Brian Grazer crashes the elevator meeting, thinks Carl Weathers is Cuba Gooding Jr., and says 'I'm gonna skip this one. Good to see you, Brian.' — then leaves.
jokes.setup: Brian Grazer joins the stuck elevator to meet the team.
jokes.punchline: Brian also mistakes Carl for Cuba Gooding Jr., then addresses Carl as 'Brian' before excusing himself — suggesting he's either confused or simply doesn't register anyone who isn't important enough to warrant accurate name recall.
jokes.explanation: The joke of BOTH Imagine principals misidentifying Carl as Cuba Gooding Jr. escalates the earlier bit nicely. Brian calling Carl 'Brian' adds an extra layer of obliviousness that's characteristically Hollywood-dismissive.

jokes.text: Michael's 'Orange County Imagine' office sign is so similar to 'Imaging' (medical) that the office receives confused medical patients.
jokes.setup: Michael is set up in his new Orange County Imagine office.
jokes.punchline: The sign 'Orange County Imagine' looks like 'Imaging' (a medical imaging center), so patients show up expecting medical services. One man says 'An embolism? I was just here to pitch a game show.' — suggesting he's a pitch-confused patient.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed visual/wordplay gag. The sign confusion has a lovely symmetry — entertainment company visitors end up at a medical facility and vice versa — with 'An embolism? I was just here to pitch a game show' being the perfect line encapsulating both confusions simultaneously.

jokes.text: 'But can I leave a sample?' — the confused medical patient being redirected to 'imaging down there.'
jokes.setup: The medical patient is being redirected to the actual imaging center.
jokes.punchline: He asks if he can 'leave a sample' at the entertainment office — either genuinely confused about the facility or offering his spec script/pitch as a 'sample.'
jokes.explanation: A double-meaning joke — 'leave a sample' works as medical (urine sample) and Hollywood (pitch/demo reel). Solid economy but character integration is low since this is a one-off patient.

jokes.text: Michael and George Sr. tussle over the Mexican porn — 'See, this is the (BLEEP) kayak all over again.'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to handle the pornography gift graciously while George pulls it back.
jokes.punchline: 'This is the (BLEEP) kayak all over again' — a reference to a presumably similar gift-related fight the audience hasn't seen, delivered with the total conviction of a long-running family grievance.
jokes.explanation: The kayak reference works as an invented callback — it implies a rich history of Michael and George Sr. fighting over gifts, specifically a kayak, that we never see. The casual delivery of an unexplained specific grievance is a comedy technique the show uses brilliantly. It suggests a lifetime of dysfunctional family history in one line.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I live in Mexico now and I don't always know how to ask exactly... for what I want. And that's almost over now.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is explaining why Mexican porn is a reasonable gift
jokes.punchline: He trails off ('don't always know how to ask exactly... for what I want') implying he can't order food properly in Spanish — but the ambiguity about what he 'wants' combined with the porn gift is suggestively dark. 'And that's almost over now' suggests he's leaving Mexico, not that he's figured out how to ask.
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s ominous ellipsis ('don't always know how to ask for what I want') next to Mexican porn creates an intentional ambiguity. 'And that's almost over now' — suggesting Mexico, not his desires — is a good dark button.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Told you so.' — immediately after Michael starts to say his father might say 'I told you so.'
jokes.setup: Michael says 'I guess I was afraid that if I admitted Sudden Valley was a failure... you might say, "I told you so."'
jokes.punchline: George Sr. says 'Told you so' before Michael can finish expressing his fear — proving the fear correct and collapsing any possible comfort Michael was hoping for.
jokes.explanation: A textbook perfectly timed character comedy beat. George Sr.'s 'Told you so' interrupts Michael mid-sentence — proving that Michael's fear was warranted and George Sr. is incapable of even the basic decency of waiting to hear the full sentence. Maximum economy (two words), perfect character integration, structurally clean. One of the best beats in the father-son dynamic.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Do you know a guy named Herbert Love?' / Michael: 'I know a lot of people, sure, 'cause I'm a big producer now.' / George Sr.: 'He ate a mouse.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. asks if Michael knows Herbert Love
jokes.punchline: Michael's pompous 'I know a lot of people' immediately deflated by George Sr.'s complete non-sequitur context for Herbert Love: 'He ate a mouse.' This provides both character information and an absurdist qualification
jokes.explanation: Three words ('He ate a mouse') following Michael's pompous name-dropping is a perfect comedic construction. George Sr.'s method of identifying Herbert Love is entirely by this single disturbing fact — as if this is the most relevant thing to know about a person — is simultaneously absurdist and a perfect George Sr. character beat. Highly quotable and economical. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I lied about being a big shot to impress this girl whose name I don't even know.' — confessing to his father.
jokes.setup: Michael is confessing his situation to his father.
jokes.punchline: The admission that he's gone through all of this — assembling a Hollywood team, trying to get releases — to impress a woman whose name he still doesn't know.
jokes.explanation: A nice crystallization of the episode's central joke — the name-unknown gag elevated to structural irony: Michael's entire arc in this episode was motivated by a woman he cannot even identify. Works as both confession and comedy.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I didn't know it was about lying to a girl. Give me the release.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has been refusing to sign the release for the movie about his family.
jokes.punchline: The moment he learns Michael is lying to impress a girl, he immediately agrees to sign. He can't relate to making a movie about himself, but he absolutely relates to lying to a woman.
jokes.explanation: A superbly character-integrated punchline. George Sr. has refused every reasonable argument for signing the release — including his son's career and the opportunity for his story to be told — but the moment it's about deceiving a woman, he signs immediately. This encapsulates George Sr.'s character perfectly: he has no interest in his family unless it serves a romantic manipulation. Structurally clean, character-specific, and the pivot from 'Go to hell' to immediate compliance is jarring in the best way.

jokes.text: Ron Howard: 'The father-son relationship I was interested in was not you and your father, it was you and your son. You're the father.' Michael: 'But that would make my son the son.' Ron: 'Bingo.'
jokes.setup: Michael assumed the movie's father-son dynamic was about him and George Sr.
jokes.punchline: Ron clarifies: Michael is the father. Michael's response — 'But that would make my son the son' — is so obvious it loops back around to being funny, and Ron's 'Bingo' confirms it.
jokes.explanation: Michael's tautological response ('that would make my son the son') is hilarious precisely because it's correct and obvious — and Ron's 'Bingo' treating it as an insight is the perfect button. Also lands with thematic weight: Michael is repeating his father's pattern, which is the show's central thesis.

jokes.text: Ron: 'Maybe the real estate business will come around soon.' Pause. 'I could never reveal that information to a non-producer.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he doesn't think he can do the movie — it would mean invading his son's privacy.
jokes.punchline: Ron suggests real estate might come around, then when Michael clearly wonders if it will, Ron delivers 'I could never reveal that information to a non-producer' — dangling insider knowledge as motivation.
jokes.explanation: Ron's manipulation of Michael — withholding information that only producers can access — is sharp Hollywood satire. The beat works because 'I could never reveal that to a non-producer' is exactly how a powerful person would casually coerce someone.

jokes.text: Rebel Alley appears at the Ealing Club playing bagpipes — Michael hears her playing and immediately changes his mind about abandoning the producer role.
jokes.setup: Michael is about to give up on being a producer.
jokes.punchline: What changes his mind is hearing Rebel's bagpipe playing from the Ealing Club — the Scottish bagpipe music being as much an irresistible lure as Ron Howard's gossip.
jokes.explanation: The bagpipe payoff is a satisfying callback — Rebel's band is the reason she was at the club she mentioned. The joke is in the ridiculousness of bagpipe music being the thing that seduces Michael.

jokes.text: The narrator on the bagpipe music: 'And like all bagpipe music, it was hard to tell if it was good music played horribly or horrible music played well.'
jokes.setup: Michael hears Rebel playing bagpipes at the Ealing Club.
jokes.punchline: The narrator delivers a philosophical observation about the nature of bagpipe music that is both true and a deadpan comment on the show's inability to evaluate what Michael is hearing.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines, and genuinely original. The observation — that bagpipe music occupies a quality uncertainty zone — is both true and precisely funny. The economy is excellent and the phrasing is elegant. Slightly lower on character integration since it's narrator-only.

jokes.text: Rebel: 'She actually dies on camera.' Michael: 'Well, make her mentally challenged and I'll thank you in my Oscar speech.'
jokes.setup: Michael is pitching the role of his dying wife to Rebel.
jokes.punchline: Rebel, in a sharp Hollywood satire moment, notes that playing a disabled character guarantees award recognition — 'make her mentally challenged and I'll thank you in my Oscar speech' — a cutting comment on the Academy's disability-story bias.
jokes.explanation: A sharp Hollywood insider joke about Oscar bait — specifically the Academy's tendency to reward performances of disability. Rebel's confidence that this formula works ('I'll thank you in my Oscar speech') is both cynical and accurate. Well-constructed and delivered.

jokes.text: Rebel: 'There's a part like that, too, only she comes in later and she's British, so she doesn't seem... No one could ever tell that she's disabled.'
jokes.setup: Michael describes the other disabled role in the movie.
jokes.punchline: The British accent renders the disability invisible to audiences — 'No one could ever tell' — a sharp satire of how British accents confer prestige and competence on any performance.
jokes.explanation: A good companion joke to the Oscar-bait line — the British accent as disability camouflage is a specific, original observation about how American audiences perceive British performances.

jokes.text: Age comparison of sons: Rebel: 'Lem's six in August.' Michael: 'Seven in July.' Narrator: 'Michael regretted the lie, so he sort of split the difference.' Michael: 'Teen. 17. In July.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Rebel compare their sons' ages. Michael's son George-Michael is 17 — very different from Rebel's 6-year-old.
jokes.punchline: Michael tries to relate by claiming his son is 7, realizes this is a lie too far, 'splits the difference' by saying '17' — which is the truth — but delivers it as though it's a compromise number.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly constructed joke. Michael lies ('Seven in July'), immediately regrets it, and the narrator's explanation — 'he sort of split the difference' — frames the truth ('Teen, 17, in July') as a diplomatic compromise rather than honesty. The absurdity of 'splitting the difference' between 6 and 17 by saying 17 is the punchline, and '17... in July' as a hedged delivery of the real number is perfect character comedy. This is one of the episode's strongest jokes.

jokes.text: Rebel: 'They grow up so fast, don't they?' — after Michael reveals his son is 17.
jokes.setup: Michael has just said his son is 17 — which to Rebel confirms her belief that Michael's son is a bit older than she thought.
jokes.punchline: Rebel's 'They grow up so fast' is a cliché comfort phrase, but here it's darkly funny because from 6 to 17 is not 'fast' — it's an entirely different life stage. She's being unintentionally ironic.
jokes.explanation: A competent button on the age lie. 'They grow up so fast' works as accidental irony but doesn't add much beyond what joke #81 already established.

jokes.text: Rebel: 'Wow, you weren't kidding when you said you had a part for me.' — after they apparently sleep together.
jokes.setup: Michael earlier told Rebel he had a part for her in his movie.
jokes.punchline: Rebel's line implies they've just been intimate, while also referencing Michael's professional pitch — using 'part' to double-mean movie role and sexual. The line confirms what the scene implies without stating it.
jokes.explanation: A functional double entendre. The 'part' wordplay works but feels slightly too cute given the rest of the episode's subtlety.

jokes.text: Michael finally sees Rebel's name — as a tattoo — after sleeping with her. Narrator: 'Oh, my God... I'm dating Ron Howard's girlfriend.' Actually, she's his daughter. 'But that's kind of worse, don't you think?'
jokes.setup: The entire episode has been building toward Michael learning the woman's name and discovering she's Ron Howard's daughter (not girlfriend).
jokes.punchline: Michael sees the name only post-intimacy, via tattoo — the name-learning gag capped by maximum awkwardness. The narrator's 'But that's kind of worse, don't you think?' confirms the daughter/girlfriend distinction makes things significantly worse.
jokes.explanation: The episode's most structurally complex payoff. Two running jokes — the unknown name and the mistress/daughter misunderstanding — both resolve in a single devastating moment: Michael finally learns her name via tattoo after sleeping with her, and the name reveals she's Ron Howard's DAUGHTER, not girlfriend. The narrator's wry 'But that's kind of worse, don't you think?' is the perfect button. This is a top-tier AD structural moment. Only higher structure could push this to true 10 territory, but at 10 for structure it's exceptional.

jokes.text: Fake 'next episode' preview: Michael goes to a doctor about a hernia — 'That is a hernia. I'm gonna send you to my guy at Hoag Hospital. They call him the Bulge Whisperer. He does 20 of these a week.'
jokes.setup: The 'next episode' preview teases what's coming for Michael.
jokes.punchline: 'The Bulge Whisperer' — a specialist nicknamed for his hernia-surgery volume. The callback is to the patients who wandered into Michael's office looking for hernia help (joke #56) — Michael now has one himself.
jokes.explanation: 'The Bulge Whisperer' is a great nickname that works both as absurdist comedy (the specificity, the Horse Whisperer riff) and as a callback. The preview format itself is a mild meta-joke.

jokes.text: POST-CREDITS PREVIEW: Andy Richter's identical quintuplet brother Rocky hosts Conan's show in his place. Conan: 'We've got a pilot who crashes planes, and you're an actor who crashes pilots.'
jokes.setup: Andy's identical quintuplet brother Rocky has taken his place on the Conan show.
jokes.punchline: Conan's line to 'Rocky' — 'you're an actor who crashes pilots' — is a wordplay joke contrasting the literal crash of the Hudson River pilot with Andy Richter's career history of appearing in failed TV pilots.
jokes.explanation: Conan's parting shot at Andy (via Rocky) — 'you're an actor who crashes pilots' — is genuinely sharp wordplay. 'Crash' pivots from literal aviation to TV pilot failure, and the character integration is near-perfect: it's specifically about Andy Richter's real career trajectory of failed shows. The quintuplet siblings are an established AD running gag from earlier seasons.

jokes.text: Rocky/Andy: 'Yeah, that's really funny. You know what else would be funny? If I ripped that red rug right off your head and turned you into Ron Howard.'
jokes.setup: Conan has just made the pilot-crashing joke at Andy's expense.
jokes.punchline: Rocky threatens to pull Conan's hair off and reveal him as bald — like Ron Howard. This merges the episode's Ron Howard thread with the Conan thread and insults both Conan's hair and Ron Howard's baldness simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: An elegant callback that ties together multiple episode threads in a single insult. The threat to expose Conan as secretly bald — 'turn you into Ron Howard' — works as a burn on Conan's vanity, as a callback to Ron Howard's prominent presence throughout the episode, and as a final bit of corporate warfare between the two worlds Michael has been navigating.

tmdb_overview: Michael is presented with a new business opportunity that could change his life, but unfortunately, he needs his family to sign off.
insight: Absurdist escalation sustains 80-point score with steady 1.33 jokes-per-minute output.
